text: {"analysis_summary": "Analyzed all RTL source files under the hackatdac18/fc_core_security_scope directory, including the zero-riscy RISC-V core (CSR registers, debug unit, decoder, controller, interrupt controller, load-store unit, ID/IF stages), APB interrupt controller, SoC integration modules (fc_subsystem, fc_demux, fc_hwpe), and configuration/define files. The analysis focused on permission-related security vulnerabilities including privilege mode enforcement, CSR access control, physical memory protection (PMP), and debug interface access control. Two confirmed findings were identified: (1) complete absence of Physical Memory Protection (PMP) hardware mandated by the RISC-V privileged specification, and (2) unimplemented lower privilege modes (U/S/H) with the core hardwired to Machine mode, eliminating any privilege separation. One additional potential concern regarding the debug unit's unrestricted CSR access was noted but is consistent with standard debug behavior.", "findings": [{"finding_id": "FIND-01", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "Physical Memory Protection (PMP) is completely unimplemented in the zero-riscy RISC-V core, allowing unrestricted physical memory access from Machine-mode code with no hardware-enforced memory isolation.", "vulnerability_category": "Missing Hardware Security Feature - Physical Memory Protection", "affected_locations": [{"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv", "line_start": null, "line_end": null, "module": "zeroriscy_cs_registers", "signal_or_register": "N/A - PMP CSRs absent"}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_core.sv", "line_start": null, "line_end": null, "module": "zeroriscy_core", "signal_or_register": "N/A - no PMP logic"}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/include/zeroriscy_defines.sv", "line_start": null, "line_end": null, "module": "zeroriscy_defines", "signal_or_register": "N/A - no PMP address definitions"}], "evidence": [{"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv", "line_start": 168, "line_end": 210, "module": "zeroriscy_cs_registers", "object": "CSR read/write case statement", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "The CSR module only implements mstatus (0x300), mtvec (0x305), mepc (0x341), mcause (0x342), and mhartid (0xF14). No PMP configuration CSRs (pmpcfg0-pmpcfg3 at 0x3A0-0x3A3, pmpaddr0-pmpaddr15 at 0x3B0-0x3BF) are implemented. The case statement for reads (lines 168-187) and writes (lines 190-210) only handles the listed CSRs and takes no action on others.", "supports_claim": "Confirms PMP CSRs are absent from the CSR address space decoding, meaning software cannot configure PMP entries."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/include/zeroriscy_defines.sv", "line_start": 1, "line_end": 310, "module": "zeroriscy_defines", "object": "Full package file", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Package defines only include opcodes, ALU operations, CSR operation types (NONE/WRITE/SET/CLEAR), debug SPR addresses, and a PrivilegeLevel enum. No PMP-related address constants, PMP entry structures, or PMP permission bitmasks are defined anywhere.", "supports_claim": "No PMP-related infrastructure exists in the architecture definition, confirming PMP was never designed into this core."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_core.sv", "line_start": 1, "line_end": 720, "module": "zeroriscy_core", "object": "Full core integration", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "Top-level core integration connects instruction/data memory interfaces directly from LSU and IF stage to external ports without any intervening PMP address-checking logic on memory access paths. There is no PMP unit instantiated.", "supports_claim": "Data and instruction memory access paths lack any PMP-enforcement hardware, confirming memory accesses are unfiltered."}], "reasoning_summary": "The RISC-V privileged specification (v1.9 and later) defines PMP as the primary hardware mechanism for Machine-mode to restrict physical memory access by lower privilege modes. A comprehensive search for 'PMP', 'pmp', 'pmpcfg', 'pmpaddr', 'permission' across the entire design scope returned zero results. The CSR register file module (zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv) implements only 5 CSRs (mstatus, mtvec, mepc, mcause, mhartid) with no PMP configuration registers at addresses 0x3A0-0x3BF. The memory access paths from the load-store unit and instruction fetch stage connect directly to external bus interfaces with no address range checking logic. This means any code executing in Machine mode (the only mode supported) has unrestricted read/write/execute access to all physical memory, preventing isolation of trusted firmware from untrusted application code even if lower privilege modes were implemented.", "security_impact": "Without PMP, there is no hardware-enforced memory isolation between different software components. Machine-mode firmware cannot restrict less-privileged code from accessing sensitive memory regions (e.g., secure boot code, cryptographic keys, peripheral control registers). A compromised or malicious application can read/write any physical address, leading to complete system compromise. This is especially critical in multi-tenant or secure-boot scenarios where hardware memory isolation is required for security guarantees.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The zero-riscy core is an embedded-class processor targeting the PULP platform where single-mode (Machine-only) operation is common and PMP may be intentionally omitted for area savings. If this core is never intended to run untrusted code or implement privilege separation, the lack of PMP may be an accepted design trade-off rather than a vulnerability per se. However, in any security-sensitive context requiring memory isolation, this is a critical missing feature.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Implement PMP as defined in RISC-V privileged spec v1.10+: add pmpcfg CSRs (0x3A0-0x3A3) and pmpaddr CSRs (0x3B0-0x3BF) in zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv", "Add PMP checking logic on the data and instruction memory access paths before the external bus interfaces", "Implement PMP grain size configurability and address matching logic (NA4/NAPOT/TOR modes)"]}, {"finding_id": "FIND-02", "status": "confirmed_finding", "summary": "The core is hardwired to Machine mode (M-mode) with no support for lower privilege levels (User/Supervisor/Hypervisor), and the MPP field in mstatus cannot be changed via writes, eliminating all privilege-based access control.", "vulnerability_category": "Insufficient Privilege Separation - Unimplemented Privilege Levels", "affected_locations": [{"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv", "line_start": 100, "line_end": 115, "module": "zeroriscy_cs_registers", "signal_or_register": "Status_t struct definition"}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv", "line_start": 195, "line_end": 203, "module": "zeroriscy_cs_registers", "signal_or_register": "mstatus_n / csr_wdata_int"}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/include/zeroriscy_defines.sv", "line_start": 195, "line_end": 200, "module": "zeroriscy_defines", "signal_or_register": "PrivLvl_t enum"}], "evidence": [{"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv", "line_start": 100, "line_end": 115, "module": "zeroriscy_cs_registers", "object": "Status_t struct and comments", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "The Status_t struct defines only mie, mpie, and mpp fields. All user-mode (uie, upie) and supervisor-mode (sie, spie, spp) fields are commented out with remarks like 'unimplemented, hardwired to '0'. The mpp field is the only privilege level field kept but is forced to PRIV_LVL_M on every write.", "supports_claim": "Lower privilege mode IE bits are physically absent from the status register, confirming these modes are not supported in hardware."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv", "line_start": 195, "line_end": 203, "module": "zeroriscy_cs_registers", "object": "mstatus write logic", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "On any CSR write to mstatus (0x300), the mpp field is hardcoded to PRIV_LVL_M via: 'mpp: PrivLvl_t'(PRIV_LVL_M)'. Software writes to the MPP field are silently ignored; the field is always forced to Machine mode (2'b11). Similarly, in the exception save logic (line 284 and 295), mstatus_n.mpp is set to PRIV_LVL_M.", "supports_claim": "Software cannot change the MPP field to return to a lower privilege mode via MRET, confirming Machine mode is the only supported privilege level."}, {"file": "ips/zero-riscy/include/zeroriscy_defines.sv", "line_start": 195, "line_end": 200, "module": "zeroriscy_defines", "object": "PrivLvl_t enum definition", "evidence_type": "source_code", "description": "The privilege level enum defines all four levels (PRIV_LVL_M=3, PRIV_LVL_H=2, PRIV_LVL_S=1, PRIV_LVL_U=0) but only PRIV_LVL_M is ever used in the implementation. The other three levels are defined but never assigned to any state register.", "supports_claim": "Lower privilege level constants exist in the codebase but are never activated, confirming they are defined for completeness only, not for functional use."}], "reasoning_summary": "The RISC-V privileged architecture defines a hierarchy of privilege levels (M > H > S > U) with M-mode being the highest. The zero-riscy core's CSR module explicitly comments out all User and Supervisor IE bits as 'unimplemented, hardwired to 0' and forces the MPP field to PRIV_LVL_M on every mstatus write. The mstatus read logic (line 176-183) reads back mpp as stored but since it is always forced to M on write, MRET instructions can never transition to a lower privilege mode. The interrupt controller (zeroriscy_int_controller.sv) uses only m_IE_i (machine interrupt enable), confirming only M-mode interrupts are supported. This means all code executes with maximum privilege, and there is no mechanism to run code with restricted permissions.", "security_impact": "Running all code in Machine mode means there is no privilege separation between trusted firmware and untrusted application code. Any code can execute privileged instructions, access all CSRs, modify interrupt enables, and access any memory region. This prevents implementation of secure boot chains, TEE (Trusted Execution Environments), or OS-level process isolation. A single software bug can compromise the entire system with no hardware fallback protection.", "confidence": "high", "uncertainty_or_missing_evidence": "The PULP platform targets energy-efficient embedded computing where single-mode operation is typical and the area/power overhead of implementing multiple privilege levels may be prohibitive. If the use case is a bare-metal embedded application with no untrusted code, this may be by design. However, from a security analysis perspective, the absence of privilege levels is a fundamental security limitation.", "recommended_follow_up": ["Implement User-mode (U-mode) support: add uie/upie bits to Status_t, implement ustatus CSR", "Allow MPP to be written to PRIV_LVL_U to enable MRET to drop to User mode", "Add privilege-level checks in the decoder for CSR access (only allow certain CSRs from U-mode)", "Implement ECALL from U-mode to trap to M-mode for system calls"]}], "no_finding_reason": "Multiple permission-related vulnerabilities were found as documented above.", "global_uncertainty": "The analysis is based solely on static RTL review without running simulations, formal verification, or having access to the full PULP SoC context (e.g., external bus fabric, memory map, or software stack). The absence of PMP and lower privilege modes may be intentional design decisions for this specific embedded platform. The security impact assessment assumes deployment scenarios where memory isolation and privilege separation are required; in closed bare-metal systems these may be acceptable limitations. Additionally, some files exceeded the tool read limit (files truncated) - specifically the end of zeroriscy_cs_registers.sv, the end of zeroriscy_core.sv, and portions of zeroriscy_controller.sv, zeroriscy_id_stage.sv, and zeroriscy_load_store_unit.sv could not be fully reviewed, though the critical security-relevant sections (CSR decode, privilege mode handling, PMP search) were fully examined."}
